data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1125 GMT - Italy's new five-year benchmark government bond, or BTP, should offer a yield 4-5 basis points higher than the previous one, says Luca Cazzulani, head of strategy at the Investment Institute by UniCredit, in a note. The Italian treasury announced Tuesday the launch of a new 2.85% February 2031 BTP alongside the reopening of three other bonds at an auction Friday. The new 2031-dated BTP should be attractive relative to BTPs with maturities in 2029, as well as relative to April 2030- and August 2030-dated BTPs, Cazzulani says. Italy's 10-year BTP yield is up 0.7 basis points at 3.585%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1040 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields fall in European midday trade. Speeches by Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell and Governor Michelle Bowman on Tuesday gave contrasting policy outlooks, says Brown Brothers Harriman's Elias Haddad in a note. Bowman suggested last week's rate cut would be the start of a series, while Powell was more cautious. "The risk is the Fed turns more dovish by the time of the December 9-10 FOMC meeting," Brown Brothers Harriman's senior markets strategist says. The two-year Treasury eases 0.7 basis point to 3.562%, the 10-year yield falls 1.4 basis points to 4.103%, while the 30-year yield is down 1.7 basis points at 4.719%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1038 GMT - The Swedish krona looks set to strengthen by year-end as the Riksbank is likely finished cutting interest rates, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. The Riksbank unexpectedly cut rates by 25 basis points on Tuesday. However, the updated rate projections indicated the end of the cutting cycle. The euro could fall back below 11.00 by year-end, he says. "In the longer run, we agree with the Riksbank's assessment that the krona remains undervalued, and this rate cut does not materially change our downward-sloping forecast for [euro versus the krona] into mid-2026." The euro rises 0.1% to 11.0208 krona. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1037 GMT - Malaysia's GDP is expected to grow 4.2% in 2025, with a strong 2H building on solid 1H momentum, RHB economists Barnabas Gan and Chin Yee Sian say. Growth will be supported by steady domestic demand, wage gains and continuing multiyear public and private projects. The economy is forecast to strengthen further in 2026, with 4.7% growth, underpinned by investment completion, higher capital deployment in strategic sectors and policies under the 13th Malaysia Plan, a roadmap for economic development. Private consumption is set to remain a growth anchor, buoyed by rising household incomes from civil service wage adjustments and a tighter labor market. While U.S. sectoral tariffs might weigh, the economists believe Malaysia's diversified services sector could cushion external shocks. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0957 GMT - Sterling looks vulnerable after Tuesday's U.K. purchasing managers' index survey missed expectations and pointed to a slowdown in economic activity, Monex Europe analysts say in a note. The downbeat survey "aligns with our view that the U.K. is on a faltering trajectory," they say. This could push the Bank of England towards cutting interest rates at a faster pace. The negative momentum from the PMI survey could persist and leave sterling at risk of falling, particularly if risk appetite sours, they say. Sterling falls 0.4% to $1.3474 against a stronger dollar. The euro falls 0.1% to 0.8729 pounds after reaching an almost two-month high of 0.8745 Tuesday, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0941 GMT - Singapore's headline inflation is expected to be subdued at 1.0% in 2025, in line with the Monetary Authority of Singapore's projections, say CGS International economists Mohamed Afham Zulghafir and Nazmi Idrus. After inflation moderated in August, CGS views the risks as balanced. While renewed shocks could drive up energy and shipping costs, softer global and domestic growth might keep core inflation low for longer. The economists add that the impact of inflationary pressures from global trade conflicts on Singapore should be cushioned by weaker external demand, which exerts a disinflationary effect. Domestically, government subsidies for essential services are also expected to help cap price increases in the services sector, they note. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0913 GMT - A weak economic growth outlook favors a slightly longer duration within bond portfolios, ODDO BHF's Laurent Denize says in a note. Bonds with longer duration are more sensitive to interest-rate changes. "Given the subdued economic growth in the U.S. and Europe, we believe a slightly longer duration makes sense," says Denize, who is co-CIO of ODDO BHF and CIO of ODDO BHF Asset Management. The asset manager has duration preference for U.S. Treasurys. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield eases 0.8 basis point to 4.109%, while the 30-year Trasury yield is down 1.2 basis points at 4.725%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0827 GMT - The euro extends its losses against the dollar after data showed German business confidence unexpectedly fell in September. The ifo's business climate index fell to 87.7 from 88.9 in August. Economists in a WSJ poll expected a reading of 89.2. The gauge for business expectations fell to 89.7 in September from 91.4 in August, below the 92.0 forecast. The current conditions index dropped to 85.7 from 86.4 against an expected 86.5. The euro falls to an intraday low of $1.1770 after the data, from $1.1784 beforehand. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0824 GMT - The Thai baht's appreciation is unlikely to pose a major risk to the economy, says Gareth Leather, senior Asia economist at Capital Economics, in a note. The baht has appreciated by 7% against the U.S. dollar since the start of this year, making it the second strongest performing currency in Asia, he notes. Policymakers are voicing fresh concerns about the baht's strength, but there is little sign that the currency is weighing on growth, he says. A stronger currency does lower the local-currency value of exports, but the link between the exchange rate and exports is weak, he says. Thai exports continue tracking other Asian economies, suggesting that global demand--and not the currency--drives performance, he adds.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - The 10-year German Bund yield edges slightly lower after Germany's Ifo business climate index misses the consensus forecast in the Wall Street Journal's poll. The Ifo business sentiment indicator for September came in at 87.7, below the WSJ consensus for 89.2. The 10-year Bund yield trades at 2.733%, down 1.8 basis points, after the data, versus 2.743% beforehand, according to Tradeweb data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0819 GMT - Chinese households' excess savings amounting to $6 trillion could underpin China's stock rally, HSBC analysts say in a research note. These savings hardly generate any income in bank accounts and could flow into different asset classes, such as equities, as investor confidence resumes, the analysts say. "This buying of local stocks by domestic investors is the real supporting factor for Chinese equities," they say. The bank no longer has a clear preference between Chinese onshore and offshore markets. After the Hong Kong market's rally of around 30% so far this year, the discount between H shares and A shares is now the smallest in five years, they note. Semiconductor names and other stocks with exposure to the rising AI adoption could be the new growth driver, they add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0811 GMT - President Trump's latest comments on the Russia-Ukraine war have raised the risk of the euro falling, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. Trump said NATO nations should shoot down Russian aircraft if they violate their airspace, adding to concerns about geopolitical risks in Europe. While his comments have had little impact on the euro, they pose "downside risks" for the single currency, he says. Trump also said Ukraine could win back all of its territory against Russia but markets are treating these remarks with caution due to a lack of progress in peace negotiations, he says. The euro falls 0.2% to $1.1793 against a stronger dollar after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell sounded cautious about cutting interest rates further. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: French utility EDF faces hurdles funding $542 billion nuclear plan, auditors say
data.description:
Key points:
  • Needs 460 billion euro investment by 2040 for its projects
  • EDF's debt, cash flow are challenges for funding, auditors say
  • Court urges cost, risk-sharing among state, EDF, customers

By Forrest Crellin and Alban Kacher

French utility EDF will need to invest some 460 billion euros ($542.39 billion) by 2040, mainly in its domestic nuclear fleet, but rising debt and cash flow issues pose major challenges, the French Court of Auditors said on Wednesday.

Nearly all of France's 57 nuclear reactors are over 30 years old and require extensive maintenance to continue operating, even as EDF plans to develop another six reactors over the next several decades.

"Everything related to ... preserving the competitiveness of the French economy, involves energy bills," Ines Mercereau, president of the Court of Auditors, said at a hearing in front of the National Assembly.

About a fifth of the needed investments will have to go into keeping the existing nuclear fleet operational until they are 60 years old, costing about 5 billion euros to 6 billion euros per year, the Court of Auditors said in a report.

EDF did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The utility is expected to finalise its plans for the new EPR2 reactors by the end of the year, allowing them to assess costs to make a final investment decision by the second half of 2026. The court estimates the total cost of the first six reactors at 75 billion euros.

EDF'S DEBT POSES A CHALLENGE TO FUND RAISING

Investing in its electricity network subsidiary Enedis is expected to cost anther 100 billion euros, as the grid will need to be modernised and reinforced, the report said.

EDF is expected to have difficulties raising capital alone for these investments due to its debt, which ballooned in 2022 during the European energy crisis, and its cash flow trajectory, the report said.

The utility has faced difficulties implementing its new long-term contract scheme to replace the old system that contracted out about a third of its annual production as plummeting market prices have hurt EDF's ability to draw clients, the report said.

EDF refused to regulate the sale of its nuclear power in the past, so its income is now more than ever linked to falling market prices, said Nicolas Goldberg, partner at Colombus Consulting.

French prices for next year delivery were still above 100 euros per megawatt-hour when these new long-term contracts were announced in late 2023, but prices have dropped sharply since then to now under 60 euros/MWh, LSEG data showed.

To address these issues, the court urged the utility to continue to monitor profitability of its renewable investments and for a clear distribution of costs and risks between the French state, EDF and its customers.

"This will not, on its own, resolve the EDF group's debt situation," said Mercereau.

($1 = 0.8481 euros)
